**Identifying Core Requirements**

I've begun dissecting the PDF, focusing on pinpointing the essential features for the React application. Currently, I'm prioritizing the core functionalities: course search, professor ratings display, user review submission, and perhaps a basic filtering system. My main objective is to establish the bare-bones features and make the application work.


**Define System Scope**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the application's boundaries. I've established the core purpose – a web-based MIS for student reviews of lecturers and courses. My focus is now on mapping out the user roles (students, admins, professors) and their corresponding functionalities. I'm prioritizing student registration (simulated), professor/course listings, search/filter capabilities, and the rating/review submission process.


**Structuring the App Components**

I'm presently building out the component structure, starting with the core files. I've got the `index.html` setup, and I'm focusing on `index.tsx` for the entry point, `App.tsx` for layout, and the interfaces in `types.ts`. I'm starting to build out a basic navigational setup for the mock data with components like `Navbar.tsx` and a `Hero.tsx` for the landing page.


**Implementing Mock Data & Routing**

I've been occupied with loading in the mock data to populate the dashboard. I've successfully set up the application-level state for professor and review data, ensuring that the "Add Review" feature will update the UI immediately. I'm also using `HashRouter` for routing as directed. I have selected `recharts` for the dashboard components, and `lucide-react` for icons.

I'm presently building out the components in more detail and considering how they interact. I'm focused on the types: `Professor`, `Review`, `Department`.  `constants.ts` will hold the mock data. I also have to start thinking about the student registration and admin dashboards.

I'm now focusing on user roles. I will use a simple "Login" toggle to manage user state (Student vs. Admin). The default will be "Guest/Student" view.  Admins will have additional stats and review deletion capabilities, while students can write reviews. I'm also planning a dedicated "Analytics" page to fulfill the MIS requirement. This will now be integrated into the dashboard. I've noted down the key dependencies I will be using in this project.


**Incorporating AI Summarization**

I've been meticulously refining the "Summarize Reviews with AI" feature. My focus is now on integrating the Gemini API for providing insightful summaries within the `ProfessorDetails` component. The `gemini-2.5-flash` model seems like the right fit for the task. I need to make sure the app handles the API key securely. I need to make sure there's robust error handling for API calls, and I'll keep the AI summary feature as a toggle that will be hidden if there is no key. This should demonstrate the integration while staying within the provided constraints.


**Enumerating App Structure**

I'm presently building out the components in more detail and considering how they interact. I'm focused on the types: `Professor`, `Review`, `Department`.  `constants.ts` will hold the mock data.  I'll manage the `professors` and `reviews` state in `App.tsx`. The `Dashboard` component is going to show analytics, with `ProfessorDetails` showcasing reviews and allowing new review submissions. I also have to start thinking about the student registration and admin dashboards. I have also finalized the component and file list for the project.

payload.text:
**Investigating Particle Effects**

I'm currently looking into how to implement a particle effect in my application. The `landonorris.com` example has provided some initial inspiration, but I need to identify the best libraries or approaches. The use of Webflow, Rive, and potentially Three.js in the source code is a starting point, and I'm evaluating their suitability for my specific needs.


**Considering Canvas-Based Effects**

I've been thinking about the best approach for the particle system. I'm leaning toward a canvas implementation, either with JavaScript or custom WebGL shaders. The landonorris.com site's use of advanced WebGL has made an impression. While a similar effect might be too complex, I want to create a slightly interactive, dynamic particle system with canvas. This should fit the theme, and be performant in the Hero component.


**Devising the Constellation Effect**

I'm now focusing on a more achievable goal: a constellation-style particle network. I've decided to build a custom canvas component, `ParticlesBackground.tsx`, to create subtle, interactive connections in the Hero section. The planned animation will feature slowly moving particles, linked by lines to nearby particles, and responding to mouse movement. This aligns with the networking theme without needing complex WebGL.


**Designing Particle Interaction**

I'm now implementing the `ParticlesBackground.tsx` component. The plan is to have subtle, light blue particles subtly move and connect, influenced by mouse position. I'm focusing on the animation loop and drawing logic now. I'll need to remove the static gradient background, or blend the blob SVG with the particles. The aim is to achieve a techy but not distracting visual element.


**Replacing Background Element**

I've decided to replace the static background element with the canvas, which should provide a cleaner visual integration for the particle effect.  I'm now focused on building the `ParticlesBackground.tsx` component, including the canvas and animation logic, and will then modify `Hero.tsx` to integrate it seamlessly.  I'm currently working on the details of `ParticlesBackground.tsx`, including `useRef` for the canvas and animation frames, handling window resizes, and mouse interaction.


**Confirming Canvas Integration**

I'm solidifying my approach: the 2D canvas particle system is definitely the right choice. I'm focusing on crafting a performant and subtle "constellation" effect using standard canvas techniques, which should integrate nicely with the existing design elements. The goal is a visually appealing, tech-themed background that feels interconnected, without needing a heavy 3D or animation library.


**Finalizing Particle Physics**

I'm presently building the `ParticlesBackground.tsx` component, including animation and interaction. I've integrated a resize handler for the canvas, and I'm adding mouse interaction and particle behavior updates, including a `Particle` class. Now, the particles will move, bounce off canvas edges, and respond to mouse proximity, with speed and size tweaks. The particles will also be drawn on the canvas now.


**Implementing Particle Connections**

I'm now focusing on drawing the connections between particles in `ParticlesBackground.tsx`. I'm calculating the distance between each pair of particles and drawing a line if they are within a certain proximity. The line's opacity will vary based on the distance. I'm also implementing the force calculation from mouse position to add a subtle interaction, making the particles repel from the cursor. I'm still working on optimizing this part of the draw loop.


**Perfecting Connection Logic**

I'm presently optimizing the drawing of particle connections. I've implemented the distance calculation and conditional line drawing based on proximity thresholds. The line opacity now varies with distance, creating a subtle visual effect. I'm fine-tuning the mouse interaction to improve the repulsion effect, making the whole animation feel more alive.  I've also integrated the animation and mouse event listeners with cleanup handlers to ensure smooth lifecycle management.


**Positioning the Canvas**

I'm now integrating the `ParticlesBackground` component into `Hero.tsx`. I realized I need to ensure the canvas takes up the full space of the hero section for proper mouse interaction, as `mousemove` relies on clientX and clientY relative to the canvas. I'm focusing on the positioning and z-index to get this working. The existing styling should prevent unwanted overflow and ensure proper layering.

**Adjusting Component Integration**

I'm now integrating the `ParticlesBackground` component into the `Hero` component. My primary focus is on ensuring the canvas fits the space correctly and takes up the full hero section. I'm removing the old CSS blobs and updating the `Hero.tsx` to include `ParticlesBackground`.

To satisfy your request for particle effects similar to the reference provided (which features sophisticated, dynamic background visuals), I will create a custom canvas-based particle system. This system will feature a "constellation" network effect where particles float and connect when close to each other, simulating a connected network of data—perfect for a "Rate My Professor" app.

Here are the changes:
1.  **New Component (`components/ParticlesBackground.tsx`)**: Implements a high-performance HTML5 Canvas animation with interacting particles and mouse repulsion/attraction.
2.  **Update `components/Hero.tsx`**: Replaces the static CSS gradient blobs with the new `ParticlesBackground` component to create a dynamic, modern opening section.

**Addressing Responsive Behavior**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that the `ParticlesBackground` component dynamically resizes to fit its container accurately. The existing code checks for a parent element and adjusts canvas dimensions accordingly. I've realized the logic is slightly flawed for cases where the component is nested within a smaller, positioned element. I'm now implementing the following: instead of relying on `window` events, I'm refactoring it to directly reference the dimensions of its immediate parent, ensuring that particles correctly adapt to any container size, no matter how the component is nested in the DOM.


**Optimizing Particle Density**

I'm now revising my thinking on how to integrate the particles. I've successfully refactored the `ParticlesBackground` component to be configurable with several new props. Specifically, I adjusted the particle density multiplier to optimize the particle count for the search box. I'm now integrating this updated component within the Hero component.  Next, I'll focus on styling the search bar container to make the particles visible.


**Visualizing Search Enhancements**

I'm now iterating on the visual design for the particle effect.  I'm exploring making the search box background `bg-white/80` with `backdrop-blur-xl`. The particles will be blue and placed behind the glass, which creates a high-tech vibe. I've updated the `ParticlesBackground` component interface to include configurable color and particle density, which are now working.


**Executing Color Adjustments**

I'm now implementing the color and density adjustments for the search box particles. The background will now use a translucent white with blur, and I've set the particle and line colors to a blue theme. The `particleCountFactor` for the search box has been fine-tuned to achieve the desired subtle effect. This looks great so far!

To implement the particle effect within the search box, I will first enhance the `ParticlesBackground` component to be more configurable (accepting color, density, and interaction settings via props). Then, I will integrate this component into the search bar in the `Hero` section, styling the input to be semi-transparent so the particles interact visibly within the field.
